summary: Tractate Yoma daf 85a covers: Shmuel's ruling: found a child - majority determines whether to violate YK; Majority of inhabitants gentile: do we save a person whose identity is unknown?; Rocks collapse on a person on Yom Kippur - remove rocks to save them; If found alive, continue removing rocks; if dead, stop; If found dead, leave them and do not continue violating YK; R. Yishmael, R. Akiva, R. Elazar ben Azaryah: scriptural basis for saving life on YK.

display.whats: Shmuel's ruling: found a child - majority determines whether to violate YK
display.hint: Shmuel rules: if a child is found in a pile of rubble on YK, whether we violate YK to save them depends on the majority composition of the local population.
display.title: Majority Rule: Rescuing a Child Under Rubble

learning.ahaMoment: The majority rule here is not about statistics - it is a legal tool that converts probability into obligation. If most residents are Jewish, the obligation to save is fully triggered.
learning.memoryAnchor: Majority Jewish population - violate YK to rescue: probability of Jewishness is enough to require action.
learning.learnerQuestion: When a child is found buried in rubble on Yom Kippur, how do we decide whether to violate the day to rescue them?
learning.coreTension: Between the obligation to save every potentially Jewish life and the reality that the rescue itself involves certain YK violations, requiring some principle to determine when to act.
learning.coreMove: Shmuel rules that the demographic majority of the local population is the operative criterion: if the majority is Jewish, we assume the buried child may be Jewish and we violate YK to rescue them. The majority functions as a legal presumption of identity.

takeaway.text: When identity is unknown and life is at stake, halakha uses the demographic majority as a legal presumption to determine whether to violate YK for rescue; probability about identity translates directly into an obligation to act.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may expect certainty of Jewish identity before violating YK; the Gemara's ruling shows that probabilistic reasoning (majority) is sufficient to trigger the life-saving obligation.

argumentFlow.label: Shmuel rereads the foundling rule as leniency
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: Shmuel's ruling about a found child concerns clearing debris off him on Shabbat. Read straight, it implies we follow the local majority even for saving a life - so in a gentile-majority town we would not clear the rubble. The Gemara rejects that reading: Shmuel was only explaining the first clause (status, e.g. lineage), and there following the majority is a leniency. For saving a life, majority does not control - we clear the debris on the chance he is alive.

theological: Majority determines: we go by statistical likelihood when we cannot know for cer
narrative: Shmuel's ruling: found a child - majority determines whether

requiresUnderstanding: Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: The majority of a town's population can decide whether we bother saving a buried person.
misconceptions.correction: Majority determines status questions like lineage, not whether to save a life. Any possibility that a person is alive obligates us to desecrate Shabbat to dig him out.

visualizableElements.name: Shmuel's ruling: found a child - majority determin

quizSeeds.question: If most of the town is gentile, may one clear Shabbat debris off a buried foundling to save his life?
quizSeeds.answer: Yes. The majority rule sets the child's status for matters like lineage, but it does not govern pikuach nefesh. To save a life we act on the mere possibility he is alive, ignoring the majority.

quizSeeds.question: What was the Gemara's mistaken reading of Shmuel, and how was it corrected?
quizSeeds.answer: The mistaken reading was that Shmuel applied 'follow the majority' even to life-saving, which would bar clearing debris in a gentile-majority town. The correction: Shmuel spoke only about the child's legal status, not about saving a life.

sugyot.finalRuling: Shmuel rules: if a child is found in a pile of rubble on YK, whether we violate YK to save them depends on the majority composition of the local popul

display.whats: Majority of inhabitants gentile: do we save a person whose identity is unknown?
display.hint: If the majority of the local population is gentile, the Gemara debates whether we still assume the buried person might be Jewish and violate YK.
display.title: Gentile Majority and the Rescue Obligation

learning.ahaMoment: The gentile-majority debate is really a debate about the limits of probabilistic reasoning: does majority determine the rule completely, or does any residual uncertainty trigger caution?
learning.memoryAnchor: Gentile majority - disputed whether to violate YK: probability cuts both ways, and the Gemara preserves both positions.
learning.learnerQuestion: If the majority of local inhabitants are gentiles, do we still violate Yom Kippur to rescue someone from rubble?
learning.coreTension: Between the general obligation to preserve every human life and the specific halakhic framework for YK violations that tracks the identity of the person being saved.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara debates whether a gentile majority removes the obligation to violate YK for rescue, or whether the non-zero possibility of a Jewish person still requires action. The debate reflects a tension between strict majority-following and cautious uncertainty.

takeaway.text: When the local majority is gentile, the obligation to violate YK for rescue becomes uncertain; the debate reflects a broader principle about how far halakha extends the benefit of the doubt when probability weighs against it.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may assume gentile majority means no violation is ever permitted; the Gemara's debate shows the question is more nuanced.

argumentFlow.label: Gentile-majority foundling: feed him non-kosher food
argumentFlow.speaker: Rav Pappa
argumentFlow.text: A foundling in a gentile-majority town counts as a gentile. Rav Pappa explains the practical stakes: we need not shield him from every prohibition and may even feed him non-kosher food, treating him as a gentile for such purposes.

argumentFlow.label: Half-and-half town: treated as a Jew for damages
argumentFlow.speaker: Reish Lakish
argumentFlow.text: In a town that is half gentile and half Jewish, the foundling is treated as a Jew - and Reish Lakish says this matters for damages. But the Gemara challenges: if a Jew's ox gored the foundling's ox, how can the foundling collect as a Jew without proving he is one? The burden of proof is on the claimant, so he should not collect.

argumentFlow.label: The case where his status as claimant helps him
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The ruling is needed for the reverse case: the foundling's tam (innocuous) ox gores a Jew's ox. A Jew pays half-damages, a gentile pays in full. Treated as a Jew, the foundling pays only half - and tells the damaged party: prove I am not a Jew if you want the other half. Here the burden falls on the one demanding more.

theological: Majority gentile: even when odds are against it being a Jew, if there is any rea
narrative: Majority of inhabitants gentile: do we save a person whose i

requiresUnderstanding: Background knowledge of majority gentile context
Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Calling the half-and-half foundling 'a Jew' helps him collect damages from others.
misconceptions.correction: It does not help him collect, since he cannot prove his status and the burden is on the claimant. It helps him as a defendant - he pays only the half-damages a Jew owes, and the victim must prove otherwise to collect more.

visualizableElements.name: Majority of inhabitants gentile: do we save a pers

quizSeeds.question: In a gentile-majority town, what is the practical consequence of treating a foundling as a gentile?
quizSeeds.answer: One need not protect him from every prohibition and may even feed him non-kosher food, as one would a gentile.

quizSeeds.question: Why does treating the half-and-half foundling as a Jew help him specifically when his ox does the goring?
quizSeeds.answer: A Jew pays only half-damages for a tam ox, while a gentile pays in full. As a presumed Jew he pays half, and he can tell the victim: bring proof I am a gentile if you want the rest. The burden of proof shifts to the victim.

sugyot.finalRuling: If the majority of the local population is gentile, the Gemara debates whether we still assume the buried person might be Jewish and violate YK.

display.whats: Rocks collapse on a person on Yom Kippur - remove rocks to save them
display.hint: A pile of rocks collapses on someone on Yom Kippur: we remove the rocks to save the person, violating YK prohibitions as necessary.
display.title: Rocks Fall on Someone: Remove Them on Yom Kippur

learning.ahaMoment: The Gemara is teaching emergency response: when you can see the danger and the person is alive, you act first and deliberate never. Delay itself becomes a violation.
learning.memoryAnchor: Rocks collapse on a person - remove them immediately: no deliberation when life is visibly at stake.
learning.learnerQuestion: If a pile of rocks collapses on a person on Yom Kippur, may we move the rocks?
learning.coreTension: Between the general prohibition on moving objects on YK (muktze, labor prohibition) and the immediate danger to a human life buried beneath.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara rules unambiguously: remove the rocks. The life-saving principle fully overrides the YK prohibitions. There is no deliberation required when the danger is obvious and immediate. Pikuach nefesh is the operative principle, and it is applied without hesitation.

takeaway.text: When a human life is visibly and immediately endangered on Yom Kippur, the relevant prohibitions are overridden without deliberation; pikuach nefesh is not a reluctant exception but the governing principle in life-threatening emergencies.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may still expect some form of deliberation or rabbinic consultation before acting; the ruling emphasizes that speed is itself required when life is at stake.

argumentFlow.label: Why does the Mishnah list three uncertainties?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Mishnah says we clear debris off a buried person despite three layers of doubt: whether he is even there, whether he is alive or dead, and whether he is gentile or Jew. The Gemara asks what the Mishnah is teaching by stacking three doubts to make one point.

argumentFlow.label: It is a 'needless to say' escalation
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Mishnah uses the 'needless to say' style, building from the obvious to the surprising. Needless to say we clear when only his presence is in doubt; even if life is in doubt we clear; and even when he is certainly a Jew but his life is in doubt we clear. The real chiddush: we clear even when it is uncertain whether the buried person is a gentile at all.

theological: Rubble on YK: the practical emergency is described in steps - you remove, you ch
narrative: Rocks collapse on a person on Yom Kippur - remove rocks to s

requiresUnderstanding: Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: The Mishnah lists three doubts because all three are equally novel.
misconceptions.correction: The first two are 'needless to say' - obviously we save. Only the last, doubt about whether the person is even a Jew, is the real innovation the Mishnah is driving toward.

visualizableElements.name: Rocks collapse on a person on Yom Kippur - remove

quizSeeds.question: What are the three uncertainties the Mishnah stacks, and which one is the real teaching?
quizSeeds.answer: Whether he is under the rubble at all, whether he is alive or dead, and whether he is gentile or Jew. The real teaching is the last: we clear the rubble even when it is uncertain whether the buried person is Jewish.

quizSeeds.question: What does the 'needless to say' (lo mivaya) style accomplish here?
quizSeeds.answer: It escalates from the obviously permitted (doubt about his presence) to the genuinely novel (doubt about his Jewish status), showing that even compounded uncertainty does not stop us from saving a possible life.

sugyot.finalRuling: A pile of rocks collapses on someone on Yom Kippur: we remove the rocks to save the person, violating YK prohibitions as necessary.

display.whats: If found alive, continue removing rocks; if dead, stop
display.hint: If we find the person alive while removing rocks, we continue until they are completely freed; if we find them dead at any point, we stop removing rocks.
display.title: Found Alive: Continue; Found Dead: Stop

learning.ahaMoment: The ruling creates a dynamic obligation: it begins on probability (might be alive) and shifts to certainty (confirmed alive) with full obligation to complete the rescue - then ends cleanly when death is confirmed.
learning.memoryAnchor: Found alive - remove all rocks; found dead at first uncover - stop. Life-saving obligation follows life.
learning.learnerQuestion: If you find the person alive while removing rubble on Yom Kippur, do you continue? What if you find them dead at the first stage?
learning.coreTension: Between the obligation to save life (which continues as long as the person is alive) and the cessation of that obligation once death is confirmed.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara rules: found alive, continue removing rocks until completely free - the life-saving obligation extends through full rescue. Found dead at an early point, stop - the YK violation no longer has justification. The life-saving override terminates when life ends.

takeaway.text: The YK override for rescue tracks the presence of life: as long as the person is alive, complete the rescue regardless of effort; the moment death is confirmed, the justification for YK violation ends and activity stops.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may think 'start removing rocks' and 'continue once alive' are the same ruling; the Gemara distinguishes the trigger (life may be present) from the continuation (life is confirmed present).

argumentFlow.label: Found alive: even for fleeting life
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Mishnah says if they find him alive they keep clearing - which seems obvious, since that is saving a life. The Gemara answers it teaches a real point: we desecrate Shabbat even if it is clear he will live only a short while (chayei sha'ah) and die soon after. A brief remnant of life is still life worth saving.

theological: Found alive, keep going; found dead, stop: the override of YK is calibrated to i
narrative: If found alive, continue removing rocks; if dead, stop

requiresUnderstanding: Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Shabbat is only set aside to save someone who has a real chance at a full life.
misconceptions.correction: Even chayei sha'ah - life that will clearly end soon - is worth desecrating Shabbat for. The value is in the life itself, not its expected length.

visualizableElements.name: If found alive, continue removing rocks; if dead,

quizSeeds.question: Why is the ruling 'if found alive, keep clearing' not simply obvious?
quizSeeds.answer: It teaches that we desecrate Shabbat even for chayei sha'ah - life that will last only a short time. Even momentary survival is reason enough to keep digging.

quizSeeds.question: What is chayei sha'ah and what does this sugya establish about it?
quizSeeds.answer: Chayei sha'ah is fleeting life, where the person will certainly die soon. The sugya establishes that saving even such fleeting life overrides Shabbat.

sugyot.finalRuling: If we find the person alive while removing rocks, we continue until they are completely freed; if we find them dead at any point, we stop removing roc

display.whats: If found dead, leave them and do not continue violating YK
display.hint: Once a person is found dead, we do not continue the YK violation for the sake of a corpse - the override is for saving life, not for honoring the dead.
display.title: The Dead Cannot Be Saved: Stop the Violation

learning.ahaMoment: The override is not a blanket permission for compassionate action - it is a specific authorization for a specific goal. When the goal is gone, the permission is gone.
learning.memoryAnchor: Dead body stops the violation: the YK override was for life, not for the dead.
learning.learnerQuestion: Why do we stop violating Yom Kippur once a buried person is confirmed dead?
learning.coreTension: Between ongoing concern for the dignity of the deceased (perhaps we should still uncover the body) and the principle that the YK override was justified only for life-saving.
learning.coreMove: The Gemara is explicit: the YK violation was authorized for life-saving, not for the dead. Once death is confirmed, there is no longer a life to save, and the justification is removed. Continued violation would now be without authorization.

takeaway.text: The YK life-saving override applies strictly to the goal of preserving life; once death is confirmed, the authorization ends and no further violation is permitted, even for the dignity of the deceased.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may think ongoing respect for the deceased justifies continuing; the Gemara strictly limits the override to life-preservation.

argumentFlow.label: Found dead: why state the obvious?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Mishnah says if they find him dead they leave him - seemingly obvious. The Gemara answers it is needed against Rabbi Yehuda ben Lakish, who held one may save a corpse from a fire on Shabbat. Even he limits that to fire, where a person, agitated over his burning dead, might come to extinguish the flames. Here in a collapse there is no such risk, so the dignity of the dead does not override Shabbat.

argumentFlow.label: How far up the body do we check for life?
argumentFlow.speaker: Baraita
argumentFlow.text: The Rabbis taught: to determine if the buried victim is alive, one checks up to the nose (no breath means dead); some say up to the heart. And even if the upper bodies in a multi-person collapse are found dead, one must not assume the lower ones are dead too - there was a case where the upper were dead and the lower alive.

argumentFlow.label: Does the nose/heart dispute track the fetus-formation dispute?
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara suggests the nose-versus-heart dispute mirrors a tannaitic dispute about where a fetus first forms: from the head (per verses on 'gozi') or from the navel (Abba Shaul). It then rejects the link: even Abba Shaul, who says formation starts at the middle, agrees that the decisive sign of life is breath in the nose, per 'all in whose nostrils was the breath of life.' Rav Pappa adds that the nose/heart practical difference applies only when digging from the feet up; digging from the head down, once you reach the nose and find no breath, you stop.

theological: Stop when dead: the dead have their own honor rights, but those do not override
narrative: If found dead, leave them and do not continue violating YK

requiresUnderstanding: Background knowledge of dead body context
Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Checking 'until the nose' versus 'until the heart' reflects a deep disagreement about what counts as life.
misconceptions.correction: For determining life, even the navel-formation view concedes breath at the nose is decisive. The practical gap only matters depending on whether you dig from the feet up or head down, as Rav Pappa explains.

visualizableElements.name: If found dead, leave them and do not continue viol

quizSeeds.question: Why isn't 'if found dead, leave him' simply obvious?
quizSeeds.answer: It is needed to reject Rabbi Yehuda ben Lakish's view that one saves a corpse from fire. That leniency applies only to fire, where panic might lead to extinguishing. In a collapse there is no such risk, so a corpse does not override Shabbat.

quizSeeds.question: What is the decisive sign of life when checking a buried victim, and what verse supports it?
quizSeeds.answer: Breath at the nose. Even Abba Shaul, who locates a fetus's formation at the navel, agrees the main sign of life is in the nose, from 'all in whose nostrils was the breath of the spirit of life' (Genesis 7:22).

quizSeeds.question: According to Rav Pappa, when does the nose-versus-heart distinction actually matter?
quizSeeds.answer: When uncovering the body from the feet upward. Then checking to the heart is not enough - one must reach the nose. But uncovering from the head down, once you check the nose and find no breath, you may stop.

sugyot.finalRuling: Once a person is found dead, we do not continue the YK violation for the sake of a corpse - the override is for saving life, not for honoring the dead

display.whats: R. Yishmael, R. Akiva, R. Elazar ben Azaryah: scriptural basis for saving life on YK
display.hint: Three Tannaim offer different scriptural sources for the principle that saving life overrides Yom Kippur - each derivation illuminates a different facet of the principle.
display.title: Three Tannaim on the Source for Life Overriding YK

learning.ahaMoment: When three major Tannaim all independently derive the same principle, the preserved disagreement about method signals certainty about the conclusion. The principle is beyond dispute; only the best derivation is debated.
learning.memoryAnchor: R. Yishmael, R. Akiva, R. Elazar ben Azaryah: three verses, one conclusion. Life overrides YK is so certain every sage found their own proof.
learning.learnerQuestion: What scriptural basis do R. Yishmael, R. Akiva, and R. Elazar ben Azaryah each use to derive that saving life overrides Yom Kippur?
learning.coreTension: Between the practical consensus (life overrides YK, obviously) and the Tannaitic need to ground this principle in an explicit scriptural derivation.
learning.coreMove: Each Tanna finds a different verse: R. Yishmael from 'the Israelites shall keep the Shabbat' (implying not at the cost of life), R. Akiva from 'do not hand over to death those unjustly condemned' (life preservation as a value), R. Elazar ben Azaryah from 'live by them' (the mitzvot enable life, not death). The preserved disagreement signals that the principle is so fundamental that every sage sought to anchor it in their own reading.

takeaway.text: The fact that three major Tannaim each offer different scriptural sources for pikuach nefesh overriding YK shows that this principle is so fundamental that every sage sought an independent anchor for it; the multiple derivations are expressions of shared conviction, not competing claims.

learning.learningBlocker: Students may ask 'which derivation is correct?' - the Gemara preserves all three because the principle is held with such certainty that multiple prooftexts are treated as reinforcing rather than competing.

argumentFlow.label: The roadside question: source for pikuach nefesh overriding Shabbat
argumentFlow.speaker: Gemara
argumentFlow.text: A famous scene: Rabbi Yishmael, Rabbi Akiva, and Rabbi Elazar ben Azarya were walking, with junior scholars behind them, when the question arose - from where do we know that saving a life overrides Shabbat? Each sage offers a scriptural proof.

argumentFlow.label: Rabbi Yishmael: kal vachomer from the burglar
argumentFlow.speaker: Rabbi Yishmael
argumentFlow.text: Rabbi Yishmael cites the thief who breaks in (Exodus 22:1): one may kill him though it is only uncertain whether he came for money or for lives, and though bloodshed defiles the land and drives away the Divine Presence. If saving the homeowner's life justifies even killing despite all that, all the more so saving a life overrides Shabbat.

argumentFlow.label: Rabbi Akiva: take him from My altar
argumentFlow.speaker: Rabbi Akiva
argumentFlow.text: Rabbi Akiva cites 'you shall take him from My altar to die' (Exodus 21:14): a murderer-priest is taken even from the altar to be executed, but if he has begun the service, 'from on top of My altar' you do not take him - he finishes first. Rabba bar bar Chana in Rabbi Yochanan's name notes this 'do not take him down' applies to executing him, setting up the next step's distinction.

theological: Three derivations: when multiple Sages offer different sources for the same prin
narrative: R. Yishmael, R. Akiva, R. Elazar ben Azaryah: scriptural bas

requiresUnderstanding: Background knowledge of R. Yishmael context
Understanding of Yom Kippur halachic framework

misconceptions.misconception: Each sage's verse is an independent, equally airtight proof that saving life overrides Shabbat.
misconceptions.correction: As 85b will show, Rava holds every proof except Shmuel's can be refuted, because they establish overriding Shabbat only for certain danger, not for the doubtful danger that is the real question.

visualizableElements.name: R. Yishmael, R. Akiva, R. Elazar ben Azaryah: scri

quizSeeds.question: What is Rabbi Yishmael's a fortiori argument that saving a life overrides Shabbat?
quizSeeds.answer: The Torah lets a homeowner kill a burglar despite only a doubt that the burglar means to kill, and despite bloodshed defiling the land and banishing the Divine Presence. If a doubtful threat to life justifies that, certainly saving a life overrides Shabbat.

quizSeeds.question: What does 'take him from My altar' (Exodus 21:14) teach according to Rabbi Akiva?
quizSeeds.answer: A murderer-priest is removed even from the altar for execution, but only before he begins the service. 'From on top of My altar' - once mid-service - he is not interrupted to be executed; that interruption is reserved, as the next sugya shows, for saving a life.

sugyot.finalRuling: Three Tannaim offer different scriptural sources for the principle that saving life overrides Yom Kippur - each derivation illuminates a different fac

rashiTranslations.he: ושמואל אמר לפקח עליו את הגל. קאמר דאם רוב ישראל
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: Shmuel - majority of inhabitants determines whether to violate YK.

rashiTranslations.he: אבל במחצה על מחצה לא וכ"ש ברוב כותים: אינו כן. דאמר
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: majority of gentiles in the area - statistical doubt still triggers the override.

rashiTranslations.he: אפילו ברוב כותים מפקחין: למאי הילכתא. הוי כותי אליבא דשמואל
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: rocks collapse on someone on YK - remove them to save the person.

rashiTranslations.he: כיון דאמר מפקחין עליו כל שכן דמצוה להחיותו ולמאי הילכתא הוי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: if found alive while removing rocks, continue the rescue.

rashiTranslations.he: כותי: להאכילו נבילות. עד שיגדל
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: if found dead, stop removing rocks - the override is for saving life only.

rashiTranslations.he: ויקבל עליו גירות: אם רוב ישראל
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi: three Tannaim cite different scriptural sources for saving life on YK.

rashiTranslations.he: ישראל למאי הילכתא. הוי ישראל
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 85a.

rashiTranslations.he: ברוב ישראל טפי ממחצה על מחצה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 85a.

rashiTranslations.he: אי שלא להאכילו נבילות במחצה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 85a.

rashiTranslations.he: על מחצה לא ספינן ליה: לנזקין.
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 85a.

rashiTranslations.he: שור של ישראל שנגח של כותי פטור
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 85a.

rashiTranslations.he: ושור של כותי שנגח של ישראל בין תם
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 85a.

rashiTranslations.he: בין מועד משלם נזק שלם ושל ישראל
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 85a.

rashiTranslations.he: שנגח של ישראל תם משלם חצי נזק
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains the opening discussion of this topic on 85a.

rashiTranslations.he: מועד משלם נזק שלם: אי נימא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: דנגחיה תורא דידן לתורא דידיה.
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: וקאמרינן דמחייבינן ליה לשלומי: נייתי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: ראיה. דישראל הוא ונשקול וכל זמן
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: שלא טבל בבית דין לשם גירות לא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: מצינן לישראל לחיובי לשלומי:
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: פלגא. משלם כישראל אידך פלגא ספק
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: הוא וזה תובעו נזק שלם כדין כותי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: אמר ליה אייתי ראיה דלאו ישראל אנא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: ושקול: מאי קאמר. כל הני ספיקי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: דקתני מאי אתו לאשמועינן: שמצילין
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: את המת. לחצר המעורבת דליכא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: איסור אלא טלטול: אבל הכא. כי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: לא שרית ליה לפקוחי מאי אית ליה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: למיעבד דתיהוי איסורא דאורייתא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi elaborates on the halachic details of this sugya.

rashiTranslations.he: דנשרי הא מקמי הך: עד היכן הוא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: בודק. אם דומה למת שאינו מזיז
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: איבריו עד היכן הוא מפקח לדעת
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: האמת: עד חוטמו. ואם אין חיות
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: בחוטמו שאינו מוציא רוח ודאי מת
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: ויניחוהו: הכי גרסינן אמר רב פפא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: מחלוקת מלמטה למעלה. מחלוקת
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: דהנך תנאי דמר אמר עד לבו ומר
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: אמר עד חוטמו מלמטה למעלה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: שמוצאו דרך מרגלותיו תחלה ובודק
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: והולך כלפי ראשו דמר אמר בלבו יש
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: להבחין אם יש בו חיות שנשמתו
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: דופקת שם ומר אמר עד חוטמו
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: דזימנין דאין חיות ניכר בלבו וניכר
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: בחוטמו: גזי נזרך. ונזר הוא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies the ruling and its application.

rashiTranslations.he: שער הראש: אפילו תימא אבא שאול.
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi glosses the term "gozi" (shear your hair): the nazir vow includes the hair of the head, which is the nazir crown. Rashi clarifies this is the term meaning the hair on top of the head.

rashiTranslations.he: נמי סבירא ליה עד חוטמו: ולוי
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi clarifies: even Abba Shaul also holds that the decisive sign of life is breath at the nose ("ad chutmo"). And Levi - Rashi notes that the sequence of this teaching was not explained to him.

rashiTranslations.he: הסדר לא פורש לי מהו: ושפיכות
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi glosses the phrase "and the sequence was not explained to me as to what it is": referring to Levi who said he had not been told the order of this passage. Rashi then glosses "sfekhut damim" (bloodshed): this is a grave sin.

rashiTranslations.he: דמים. שהותר לבעל בית לשפוך
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi glosses "she-hutar la-ba'al bayit lishpokh" (the homeowner is permitted to shed the blood of the burglar): the Torah permits the homeowner to kill a burglar who breaks in, to protect himself from the danger to his life.

rashiTranslations.he: דמו של זה להצלת ספק נפשו עבירה
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi glosses "damo shel zeh lehatzalat safek nafsho" (this one's blood for the sake of saving his own life which is in doubt): killing the burglar is permitted even though the threat to the homeowner is only doubtful, not certain. An aveira (transgression) is involved.

rashiTranslations.he: חמורה היא שמטמאת את הארץ
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi glosses "chamura hi she-metama'at et ha-aretz" (it is a severe sin that defiles the land): bloodshed defiles the Land of Israel, as written in the Torah.

rashiTranslations.he: כדכתיב (במדבר לה) ולא תטמא את
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi cites the verse: "v'lo titame et ha-aretz" (and do not defile the land) - referring to the section in Bamidbar (Numbers 35) about murderers. The prohibition against defiling the land through bloodshed is derived from this verse.

rashiTranslations.he: הארץ גבי פרשת רוצחין: וגורם
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi glosses "gabi parshat rotzchim" (in the section about murderers, Numbers 35): the verse about not defiling the land appears in the passage dealing with homicide law.

rashiTranslations.he: לשכינה שתסתלק. כדכתיב (שם)
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi glosses "ve-gorem la-Shekhinah she-tistalek" (and causes the Divine Presence to depart): bloodshed drives away the Shekhinah, as written in the verse.

rashiTranslations.he: אשר אני שוכן בתוכה הא אם תטמאו
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi cites the verse: "asher ani shokhein betokhah" (in which I dwell within them) - implying that if they defile the land, God will no longer dwell there. This verse is written in the context of murder.

rashiTranslations.he: איני שוכן וגבי רוצח כתיב: מעם
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi glosses "me'im mizbachi" (from My altar): if the murderer is a kohen who has come to perform the Temple service, he is removed from the altar for execution.

rashiTranslations.he: מזבחי. אם כהן הוא שבא לעבוד
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains "me'im mizbachi" further: this applies when a kohen came to perform the Temple service (avoda).

rashiTranslations.he: עבודה: ולא מעל מזבחי.  אם
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi glosses "ve-lo me'al mizbachi" (but not from on top of My altar): if he has already begun the Temple service, he is not interrupted mid-service to be brought to judgment.

rashiTranslations.he: התחיל בעבודה אין מפסיק לבא
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi explains: if the kohen-murderer has already begun the Temple service, we do not stop him mid-avoda to bring him to trial. He completes his service first.

rashiTranslations.he: לידון אלא משלים עבודתו:
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi glosses "ella mashlim avodato" (but he completes his service): the murderer-kohen who is mid-service finishes performing the Temple service before being taken for execution.

rashiTranslations.he: אבל
rashiTranslations.en: Rashi glosses "aval" (but): this introduces the contrasting ruling that follows.
